questions.question: What is an Access Point (AP)?
options: A wireless device that connects wireless clients to a wired distribution system
A mesh AP that bridges traffic over a wireless backhaul
An AP operating as a wireless client to bridge wired devices
A directional antenna used to connect sites over long distances
questions.explanation: An Access Point (AP) is a wireless device that connects wireless clients to a wired distribution system.

questions.question: What does SSID stand for?
options: Service Set Identifier
Signal Set Indicator
Station Service Identifier
Secure Session ID
questions.explanation: SSID stands for Service Set Identifier — the human-readable wireless network name.

questions.question: What is the BSSID?
options: Basic Service Set Identifier — the unique MAC address of the AP's radio
Basic Service Set Indicator — the human-readable network name
Broadcast Service Set Identifier — the multicast group of the AP
Backbone Service Set Identifier — the wired-side MAC of the AP
questions.explanation: BSSID is the Basic Service Set Identifier — the unique MAC address of the AP's radio.

questions.question: What does CSMA/CA stand for?
options: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance
Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Arbitration
Channel Sense Multiple Access with Clear Authorization
Carrier Session Management Access / Collision Avoidance
questions.explanation: CSMA/CA is Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance — the wireless media access method that avoids collisions by waiting for a clear channel.

questions.question: What are the non-overlapping 2.4 GHz channels in North America?
questions.explanation: Non-overlapping channels in 2.4 GHz (North America) are 1, 6, and 11.

questions.question: Which wireless phenomenon refers to a signal bouncing off a material such as metal?
options: Reflection
Refraction
Diffraction
Absorption
questions.explanation: Reflection is when a signal bounces off a material (e.g., metal). Refraction bends through a medium; diffraction bends around obstacles; absorption turns signal to heat.

questions.question: Which standard is Wi-Fi 6?
questions.explanation: 802.11ax is Wi-Fi 6. 802.11ac is Wi-Fi 5; 802.11n is Wi-Fi 4.

questions.question: What is an ESS?
options: Extended Service Set — multiple BSSs joined by a wired distribution system, sharing one SSID
Enhanced Security Set — a WPA3-secured BSS
External Service Set — wireless bridge between sites
Encrypted Session Set — a VPN-wrapped wireless session
questions.explanation: ESS (Extended Service Set) is multiple BSSs joined by a wired distribution system, sharing one SSID.

questions.question: What is roaming in wireless?
options: A client moving between APs in an ESS without losing the connection
A client switching between 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands
An AP rebroadcasting another AP's signal
A site-to-site wireless bridge reconnecting
questions.explanation: Roaming is a client moving between APs in an ESS without losing the connection.

questions.question: Which statement compares the 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands?
options: 2.4 GHz has better range and wall penetration but more interference; 5 GHz has less interference and non-overlapping channels
5 GHz has better range; 2.4 GHz has faster speeds
Both bands have the same interference and channel count
5 GHz penetrates walls better than 2.4 GHz
questions.explanation: 2.4 GHz has better range and wall penetration but more interference. 5 GHz has less interference and non-overlapping channels.

questions.question: What is a wireless repeater?
options: An AP that retransmits another AP's signal to extend the BSS; a single-radio repeater halves throughput
An AP that bridges wired devices into the wireless network
An outdoor AP with directional antennas for long-distance links
A mesh AP with a wired connection to the distribution system
questions.explanation: A wireless repeater is an AP that retransmits another AP's signal to extend the BSS; a single-radio repeater halves throughput.

questions.question: What is a RAP in a mesh wireless deployment?
options: Root Access Point — mesh AP with a wired connection to the distribution system
Remote Access Point — an AP serving remote VPN clients
Roaming Access Point — AP that follows mobile clients
Relay Access Point — passive signal repeater
questions.explanation: A RAP (Root Access Point) is the mesh AP with a wired connection to the distribution system. A MAP (Mesh AP) bridges traffic over the wireless backhaul.

questions.question: What is channel bonding?
options: Combining adjacent channels to increase throughput
Pairing two APs to share a single channel
Locking a client to one channel to reduce roaming
Encrypting the channel between AP and client
questions.explanation: Channel bonding is combining adjacent channels to increase throughput.

questions.question: Which term describes an AP operating as a wireless client to bridge wired devices into the wireless network?
options: Workgroup bridge (WGB / uWGB)
Wireless repeater
Outdoor bridge
Mesh Access Point (MAP)
questions.explanation: A workgroup bridge (WGB / uWGB) is an AP operating as a wireless client to bridge wired devices into the wireless network.
